title: Sporting Firearms Access Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill makes it easier and faster to bring sporting guns and bullets into the United States from other countries. It tells the Attorney General to make quicker decisions about whether to allow these imports. If someone's request to import sporting guns or bullets gets denied, the bill gives them a way to ask a court to review that decision. The goal is to speed up the process for people who want to bring in guns and ammunition for sports like hunting or target shooting.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Sporting Firearms Access Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Importation of Firearms and Ammunition
sections.text: This section changes the rules about bringing guns and bullets into the United States from other countries. The Attorney General is the person who decides whether to allow these imports. For guns and bullets that are suitable for sporting purposes like hunting or target shooting, new rules apply. Sporting purposes means activities like hunting, recreational target shooting, organized competitions, or civilian rifle match competitions. When someone asks to import a gun or bullets for sporting use, the Attorney General has ninety days to make a decision. If no decision is made within ninety days, the import is automatically allowed. If the Attorney General says no to an import request, they must explain why in writing and post that explanation on the Bureau of Alcohol Tobacco Firearms and Explosives website. The Attorney General cannot refuse to allow imports of guns or bullets that are very similar to ones already being sold in the United States. The Attorney General must work with gun industry experts to define what very similar means. This includes hunting guides, magazine editors, game commissioners, shooting groups, law enforcement, gun industry members, and other interested groups. If an import request is denied, the person who made the request can ask for a hearing to review that decision. The hearing must be held in a place that is convenient for the person making the request. After the hearing, if the Attorney General still says no, the person can take the case to federal court within sixty days. In court, the Attorney General must prove that the gun or bullets are not suitable for sporting purposes. If the court decides the Attorney General was wrong to deny the import, the court will order the Attorney General to allow it and will make the government pay the person's lawyer fees. The section also makes small changes to other parts of the law to match these new rules and adds descriptive headings to different parts of the import law.
